channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: Anthropic wants to slow AI down and Bernie wants 50%: JCal Reacts | E2297
gist: Calacanis and co-host Lon Harris react to Anthropic's call for a global AI pause and Bernie Sanders' proposal for a 50% one-time stock tax on AI companies, predicting AI job displacement becomes the defining 2028 election issue; they also dissect ComfyUI's open-source AI-video business, the persevere-vs-pivot question for startups, and the rise of cheap YouTuber-made movies.

themes.theme: AI politics: the coming 2028 backlash and equity-redistribution fight

insights.claim: Calacanis predicts the 2028 US presidential election will be decided primarily by AI job displacement, displacing inflation and wars as the number one issue.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Frames a multi-year political-risk overhang for AI-exposed equities; watch for regulatory and tax proposals targeting AI labs ahead of 2028.
insights.quote: 2028 the number one issue in the election. It's not going to be inflation. It's not going to be wars. It's going to be AI.

insights.claim: Bernie Sanders plans to introduce the American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act, a one-time 50% tax on the stock (not profits) of the largest US AI companies to give the public a direct ownership stake.
insights.speaker: Bernie Sanders (quoted)
insights.so_what: A concrete legislative threat to AI-company cap tables; even as an opening negotiating position it signals rising political appetite to claw back AI gains.
insights.quote: a onetime 50% tax not on profits but on stock

insights.claim: Calacanis predicts Anthropic and OpenAI will negotiate down from Sanders' 50% demand and voluntarily commit roughly 10% of equity to public-benefit vehicles like Invest America/Trump accounts or job retraining.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Implies a partial-dilution outcome rather than nationalization; a 10% public stake is materially less dilutive than headlines suggest.
insights.quote: I think Anthropic and OpenAI are going to sit down with Bernie Sanders and say we're willing to put 10% of our equity

insights.claim: Calacanis argues Sanders' IP-theft framing has broad bipartisan appeal because AI labs demonstrably trained on copyrighted works without permission, evidenced by numerous lawsuits and settlements.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Suggests anti-AI sentiment can unite left and MAGA-right, raising odds of punitive AI legislation regardless of party in power.

insights.claim: Current US legal precedent allows training AI on legally-acquired copyrighted material but treats infringing outputs as actionable, while training on pirated/bootlegged content (e.g., Anthropic's bootlegged book archive) remains illegal.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Defines the litigation boundary investors should watch; labs with clean licensing deals carry less legal tail risk than those relying on scraped pirated data.

insights.claim: The political right is fracturing into a pro-AI accelerationist camp (JD Vance, Peter Thiel, David Sacks) and a skeptical traditional-MAGA camp (Steve Bannon) worried about jobs and concentration of wealth.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Signals AI policy will not be a reliable partisan vote even under Republican control, increasing regulatory uncertainty for AI capex.

insights.claim: Calacanis advocates replacing complex means-tested social programs (SNAP, SSI, Medicaid, unemployment) with a simple sliding-scale UBI funded by AI profits, arguing administration and fraud consume well over half of current program dollars.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Outlines the 'grand bargain' policy structure that could channel AI wealth, relevant to how AI-tax revenue might be deployed.

insights.claim: Sam Altman's OpenResearch three-year UBI study gave 1,000 low-income adults $1,000/month and found recipients worked slightly fewer hours but valued work more and made better long-term career decisions rather than quitting.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis / Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Empirical ammunition for the UBI policy debate that AI leaders are funding; suggests labor-supply effects of cash transfers are modest.

themes.theme: Anthropic's pause call and AI-lab strategy

insights.claim: Anthropic publicly stated in a Thursday blog post that slowing the pace of global AI development would 'likely be a good thing,' warning that models show recursive self-improvement without human intervention.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: A frontier lab publicly endorsing a pause—just before a potential IPO—is unusual and may be read as competitive maneuvering or genuine alignment concern.

insights.claim: The hosts find Anthropic's pause call contradictory and self-serving given it comes on the verge of an IPO and while Anthropic continues to ship Claude, suggesting the timing is strategically bizarre.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Skepticism about a pause call may cap any narrative benefit and feed perceptions that pause rhetoric is regulatory capture to slow competitors.
insights.quote: shut down Claude then if it's too dangerous. If not, what are you doing? You're the ones behind all of this development.

insights.claim: Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora told Calacanis that Anthropic's models found internal attack vectors and bugs at a level of performance the firm had not previously seen in cybersecurity.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Real enterprise validation of frontier-model capability in cybersecurity; supports the AI-for-security thesis and Anthropic's enterprise traction.

themes.theme: ComfyUI and the open-source generative-video stack

insights.claim: ComfyUI raised a $30M round from David Sacks' Craft Ventures at a $500M post-money valuation and positions itself as an inference engine / operating system for visual generative AI rather than a model wrapper.
insights.speaker: Yoland Yen (CEO)
insights.so_what: A pricing benchmark for the gen-AI tooling layer; the inference-engine moat argument is the bull case against 'just a wrapper' criticism.
insights.quote: we raised from craft most recently 30 million round at 500 million post

insights.claim: ComfyUI's node-based control was used to produce recent AI Super Bowl ads (including Coca-Cola) and for upscaling/outpainting on the Wizard of Oz Las Vegas Sphere show, demonstrating production-grade demand for granular AI-video control.
insights.speaker: Yoland Yen
insights.so_what: Evidence that professional studios are paying for controllable AI video, validating a vertical tooling business above frontier models.

insights.claim: The hosts argue the real magic of AI workflows is multi-model chaining—using one model (e.g., Claude) to write structured prompts that then drive image models like Nano Banana or Ideogram—a workflow most casual users miss.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris / Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Reinforces that no single model wins; orchestration and tooling layers capture durable value, favoring workflow startups.
insights.quote: the number one job of AI is to write the prompt

themes.theme: Founder strategy: persevere vs pivot and the wrapper debate

insights.claim: Calacanis argues founders in big markets with a lead and remaining cash should persevere and pivot ~90% of the time rather than return capital, since returned money typically yields only 10-30 cents on the dollar after costs.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A reusable investor heuristic for evaluating shutdown-vs-pivot decisions and where to allocate follow-on conviction.
insights.quote: the money's already been pock committed. Let's play the hand.

insights.claim: Calacanis recounts that Roam Around, an AI travel planner in his accelerator's 2023 batch 28, returned its investment fearing it was 'just a ChatGPT wrapper,' was later acquired by Layla, and argues wrappers (like a Cadillac wrapping a Corvette engine) can be defensible brands.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Counters reflexive 'wrapper' dismissals; brand, UX and human-in-the-loop features can sustain a business on top of foundation models.

insights.claim: Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky is founding a new AI lab focused on user interaction and design for travel, betting people want a bespoke designed travel experience rather than a chatbot conversation; he will stay Airbnb CEO and not run the lab.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Signals a design-first AI model thesis and potential Airbnb conflict-of-interest/strategic-investment questions worth watching for shareholders.

insights.claim: Calacanis advises AI startups to build sales teams in lower-cost metros (Phoenix, Salt Lake City, Austin, Dallas, Las Vegas) where grinder salespeople accept low base plus high commission, rather than overpaying entitled SF sales execs.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Operational margin lever for scaling startups; cites Yelp, HubSpot, Spotify, and Zappos as precedents for cheaper, motivated sales talent.
insights.quote: they would prefer to get 75K and 8% of sales. In other words, they'd rather bet on themselves

insights.claim: The horror film 'Obsession' from YouTuber Curry Barker, made for ~$750,000 with Blumhouse, has grossed over $166M globally—roughly 166x its budget—signaling viable ultra-low-budget theatrical economics.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Demonstrates outsized ROI in micro-budget genre films distributed theatrically; a thesis Calacanis says he wants to invest in via a studio.
insights.quote: it's so far grossed over 166 million globally at the box office

insights.claim: Calacanis argues internet-native content (YouTube creepypastas like 'Backrooms') is now valid theatrical IP, broadening the definition of IP beyond established franchises and reviving the movie industry via Gen Z creators.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Points to a new IP-sourcing pipeline and disruption opportunity for studios and self-distributors like Terrifier's Dark Age Cinema.
